core_takeaway: Agent_1 anchored Pencil early and kept it, but the equal final outcome came mainly from complementary single-agent priorities and Agent_3's balanced proposal, not a clear first-position process advantage.
why_or_counterexample: Although Agent_1 spoke first and made Pencil the default protected item, Agent_3 as the last speaker proposed the allocation that won unanimous support. Agent_1's own proposal was rejected by the other two agents because it ignored Agent_3's Apple payoff, so first position did not translate into superior outcome or proposal control.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by emphasizing Pencil, Agent_2 accepted that if Stone/Jewel were protected, and Agent_3 supplied a concrete milestone plan giving each agent its highest-value bundle.
phase_notes.private_thinking: All three private-thinking entries converged on the same baseline: Pencil for Agent_1, Stone/Jewel for Agent_2, Apple for Agent_3, with logs and milestones as governance.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Agent_1 proposed a self-maximizing allocation that left Agent_3 empty and failed; Agent_3 proposed the balanced allocation that became the unanimous consensus.
phase_notes.private_voting: Voting was payoff-driven: Agent_2 and Agent_3 rejected Proposal #1, while all three accepted Proposal #3 because it preserved each agent's priority items.
phase_notes.reflection: No reflection section or reflection entries were present.

mechanisms.theme: Complementary valuations made an equal outcome easy
mechanisms.explanation: Each agent's highest-value items were different, so the final allocation could give everyone 100 without requiring Agent_1 to outmaneuver the last speaker.

mechanisms.theme: Agent_1's first anchor protected Pencil but did not create a decisive edge
mechanisms.explanation: The opening speaker successfully made Pencil retention a shared premise, but that premise was acceptable because Pencil had no value to the other agents.

evidence.quote: Pencil: top priority (value is basically 98 to me).
Round 1 end: Pencil stays with Agent_1.
keep Pencil with Agent_1 longer and adjust only non-Pencil transfers

mechanisms.theme: The last speaker converted alignment into the winning proposal
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_3 used the final discussion turn to articulate a concrete balanced plan, then submitted the proposal that matched the final allocation and won unanimously.

mechanisms.theme: Agent_1's own proposal failed because it was too one-sided
mechanisms.explanation: The first speaker's formal proposal gave Agent_1 full utility but left Agent_3 with nothing, so both later-position agents rejected it.

evidence.quote: Proposal 1 crowds Apple to Agent_2 and leaves Agent_3 without any items

mechanisms.theme: Private voting followed utility and mutual fit rather than speaker order
mechanisms.explanation: Agents accepted proposals that secured their own priority bundles and rejected alternatives that reduced payoff or undermined the shared baseline, regardless of who spoke first.

evidence.quote: Gives Agent_1 only Pencil (98 utility), which is inferior
It's the cleanest alignment with the agreed baseline
Proposal 3 also delivers Apple to me in Round 1
